2603.08448.title: A prospective clinical feasibility study of a conversational diagnostic AI in an ambulatory primary care clinic
2603.08448.paper_summary: Large language model (LLM)-based AI systems have shown promise for patient-facing diagnostic and management conversations in simulated settings. Translating these systems into clinical practice requires assessment in real-world workflows with rigorous safety oversight. We report a prospective, single-arm feasibility study of an LLM-based conversational AI, the Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer (AMIE), conducting clinical history taking and presentation of potential diagnoses for patients to discuss with their provider at urgent care appointments at a leading academic medical center. 100 adult patients completed an AMIE text-chat interaction up to 5 days before their appointment. We sought to assess the conversational safety and quality, patient and clinician experience, and clinical reasoning capabilities compared to primary care providers (PCPs). Human safety supervisors monitored all patient-AMIE interactions in real time and did not need to intervene to stop any consultations based on pre-defined criteria. Patients reported high satisfaction and their attitudes towards AI improved after interacting with AMIE (p < 0.001). PCPs found AMIE's output useful with a positive impact on preparedness. AMIE's differential diagnosis (DDx) included the final diagnosis, per chart review 8 weeks post-encounter, in 90% of cases, with 75% top-3 accuracy. Blinded assessment of AMIE and PCP DDx and management (Mx) plans suggested similar overall DDx and Mx plan quality, without significant differences for DDx (p = 0.6) and appropriateness and safety of Mx (p = 0.1 and 1.0, respectively). PCPs outperformed AMIE in the practicality (p = 0.003) and cost effectiveness (p = 0.004) of Mx. While further research is needed, this study demonstrates the initial feasibility, safety, and user acceptance of conversational AI in a real-world setting, representing crucial steps towards clinical translation.
2603.08448.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLM）基礎的人工智慧系統在模擬環境中顯示出對於面向患者的診斷和管理對話的潛力。將這些系統轉化為臨床實踐需要在現實工作流程中進行評估，並進行嚴格的安全監督。我們報告了一項前瞻性、單臂的可行性研究，研究對象為基於LLM的對話式人工智慧——Articulate Medical Intelligence Explorer（AMIE），該系統在一所領先的學術醫療中心進行臨床病史收集和潛在診斷的呈現，以便患者在急診就診時與其醫療提供者討論。100名成人患者在他們的約診前最多完成了5天的AMIE文本聊天互動。我們旨在評估對話的安全性和質量、患者和臨床醫師的體驗，以及與初級護理提供者（PCPs）相比的臨床推理能力。人類安全監督員實時監控所有患者與AMIE的互動，並根據預先定義的標準不需要介入停止任何諮詢。患者報告了高滿意度，並且在與AMIE互動後，他們對人工智慧的態度有所改善（p < 0.001）。PCPs認為AMIE的輸出有用，並對準備工作產生了積極影響。根據8週後的圖表回顧，AMIE的鑑別診斷（DDx）在90%的案例中包含了最終診斷，且前3名的準確率為75%。對AMIE和PCP的DDx及管理（Mx）計劃的盲評估顯示，整體DDx和Mx計劃質量相似，DDx（p = 0.6）及Mx的適當性和安全性（分別為p = 0.1和1.0）沒有顯著差異。PCPs在Mx的實用性（p = 0.003）和成本效益（p = 0.004）方面表現優於AMIE。雖然需要進一步的研究，但這項研究展示了對話式人工智慧在現實環境中的初步可行性、安全性和用戶接受度，代表了臨床轉化的重要步驟。
